sentences.text: It was just our own family, in other words, my mother, my father, my sister and two brothers, just us.
It was very difficult to get any conveyance to go.
You can imagine the chaos.
It's very difficult to describe it.
But we had a man who used to work for us, and-- Mr.
Socha, S-O-C-H-A, Joseph Socha.
And he had-- he used to transport glass for us.
So he came and he picked us up.
And as I told you, my uncle-- we were going to the train station, but my father was never finished with his work, you know.
So by the time we got to train station, the train station was bombed completely.
And my uncle managed to get away with his family, east, and we did not.
So we had to turn back home, went to see this Mr.
Socha.
And he picked us up and took us.
And on the road to that-- to where it's the direction of Dynow, eastward-- on the road, if you remember the movie, if you have seen it, it was exactly the way it is described.
We were strafed.
Stukas would run down, strafe.
We would run into the forest, back and forth.
We finally made it to that little town and we stopped there.
Well, before we left, my father had some gold dollar coins.
So he gave it out to my cousins, to everybody, a coin to take with them.
And he took everything along with him in his pockets, you know, all the monies that he had.
But you couldn't take the monies out of the bank.
Now, we had money in the bank, because the banks closed.
And also, prior to World War II, in our glass business, there was a price war on glass, because glass used to be delivered from a Belgian company in Poland.
And then a Polish company started machine glass.
Before that was hand-blown glass, where you looked in through a glass window, you saw 15 faces.
That was very cheap glass.
Machine glass was expensive.
So there was a second factory that opened up and competed with the Belgian company.
So what we decided is since it was very cheap, one of them would have to go broke, because they couldn't afford to sell it for the price of selling it.
So we loaded our warehouse full of glass.
It was absolutely full.
All the monies was spent, invested in the business.
But whatever monies we had left, you know, we just stuffed our pockets with that money and left for this town in the direction of Dynow.
And there we got stuck, because the Germans were just right behind us.
They came in into our town from Slovakia, from the south.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] there we stayed.
And we were accommodated at a local individual's house.
There were many other people running, of course.
It was very chaotic.
We slept on the floor.
And to be quite frank, you know, being children, it was rather very exotic.
It was fun to sleep on the floor with many other people there and so on.
And the gentleman whose house we stayed had a store selling flour and all kinds of such things.
And you know, that was hard to get now, because people were hoarding.
And he distributed all that.
We got a few pounds.
Everyone got a few pounds.
He gave away everything he had.
And we stayed there at that house.
Then we moved to another house with a straw roof that you had to bend down to get in there, to a Jewish fellow, an orthodox Jewish fellow, who used to buy glass from us.
So we stayed at his house until the German armies marched in.

When they marched in, you know, highly motorized-- something you didn't see in the Polish army, which was mostly you know, on horses, or-- they didn't have the-- a mechanized army like the Germans did.
When they came, it was very impressive.
Here power coming in all of a sudden.
And nothing happened.
When they first came in, it was very quiet.
We used to go out, stand around, watch them, and so on.
And as soon as they marched in, my father decided with a few other people from our town to go back to see what happened.

We had my uncle Haim-- the one who said that they won't marinate me, I'm not going to go, I know Germans, they are wonderful, the most they can do is put me to work, I've worked all my life anyway-- he stayed in our house, because he lived a little bit out of town and he thought he's safer to stay right in the center, so he stayed in our apartment and he watched our place.
My father decided to go back.

But instead of going back straight into my hometown, Krosno, they stopped in a little shtetl, a little tiny town, about 5 kilometers to the north of the town, to sort of find out, feel out what goes on in our town.
Now, we stayed behind in that town, Dynow.
And a few days later, the SS marched in.
Now, then all hell broke loose.
And I remember I was sitting with my mother and my sister, my two brothers, in that room there at-- at this gentleman's house.
Well, all of a sudden, we heard screaming outside, loud.
Jews out.
Jews out.
[NON-ENGLISH] All men out.
Men, only.
See, they were still sort of a little civilized at that time.
They were looking only for men.
They left the women, oldsters, and children alone.
And all of a sudden, one of the SS men in a SS helmet, with his gun drawn, kicks the door open into the house.
And he comes in.
He's screaming.
Where are all the men?
Out.

My mother, when she heard all this-- you know, my mother spoke German, because you see, they went to-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] this was Austria-Hungary, so this was Austria before I was born, see, so they knew German.
And so she gave me a book to read when she heard the commotion.
And I wore shorts.
It was summer, you know.
So I looked young.
And my mother asked him, what about him, pointing to me.
And he asked her, how old is he?
And my mother said, he's 14.
I was actually 16.
She was sharp enough to tell him I was 14.
So he let me sit there.
He goes into the next room and there was a boy of about my age.
He was a little taller, a local kid.
Him he took out.
And they were all taken out.
Within an hour's time, there was commotion again.
And the temple, which was a beautiful temple-- it was really a-- a Gothic structure, beautiful temple unlike what we had in our town.
Our town-- our temple was nothing to brag about.
But that one was a real Gothic, old temple, was put to the torch.
And we heard screaming and so on.
And we were all, of course, scared.
And we just sat out that night.
The following morning, around 5:00 in the morning or so, someone knocks on the door.
And that kid comes back, as pale as a sheet.
What happened?
He describes what happened.
They were all taken out to the central square.
They were taken out to the woods and they were all shot.
He dropped to the ground.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] Other dead fell on him.
And when they all left, he came to, got up, and he came back.
Within about an hour's time, another man comes back into our house, Mr.
Shamrov.
Mr.
Shamrov had a bike store in our town.
He came back.
He had a bullet in his chest.
He described exactly what happened.
When I was in Israel a few years ago, another individual by the name of Palent, Mr.
Palent, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] he told me-- he's a little older than myself.
And he told me that he was called-- he was also in that marketplace.
But they took out about a dozen or so to clean the latrines in the school where they were quartered, he said.
And about around midnight, a bunch of them came back, the SS, and chased them out.
He said he was lucky he survived or he would have been killed the same way.
Now, two of my cousins, the red Jo and the white Jo-- I had a number of uncles.
You must know that in the old country, in those days, we weren't as mobile as you are today.
Today you are born here, you die somewheres else, and you have been to a dozen places in between.
In my days, you were born in the same town.
You died in that town.
And so I had a number of uncles.
So Uncle Mordechai had a number of children.
And his name-- son, by the name of Jo, we called him the red Jo, because he was a redhead.
And Uncle Abraham, a picture of which you will see later, had all blond kids.
And we-- also Jo, we called him the white Jo.
And another one, Jo also by the name, a first cousin [BACKGROUND NOISE] of mine, that was Uncle Haim's son, the one that survived Auschwitz.
We called him the black Jo, because of his dark head.
They were all going together.
And they went further eastwards.
And they were-- it was a Friday, and my-- most of them wanted to continue going.
But the red Jo and the white Jo said, no, they have to go to the prayers.
It was Friday evening.
So they stayed back.
The others went further east.
And the same fate befell them as this kid there.
They came into town.
They took them out.
And they were killed.
They were shot there.
Now-- in that town.
The town's name was [INAUDIBLE].
And it wasn't until about oh, five, six months later, that we found that out, when we came back to our home town.
And the Jewish community petitioned the Gestapo whether they would allow us to give him a burial there.
And they said they will only allow it if my uncle will sign, and the people whose kids were killed, that they were killed by bombing, not executed.
So my uncle signed.
And they were brought to our town, and they were buried in the cemetery.
Now, but my cousin, the one that's in the Polish army, and my cousin who just died a year ago, black Jo, told me that story.
And of course, I know, because my Aunt Hentsha, you know, was continually crying.
She never stopped crying.
And that was-- they didn't want to go further east.
They had to go to the prayers.
So that was that day.
Now, my father, as I told you, went back.
And he stopped in a small town.
And we had it so arranged that he will send back a note with the man who took him there to tell us what it looks like and whether we should come back to our hometown.
And I remember that day.
I remember just exactly the way it was.
My mother was reading the note.
And in that note, he described the ordeal that he went through going there.
They cut his beard.
They took his money away.
And some were killed.
He was left-- he was let go.
And I remember his quotation.
He quoted a passage from one of the prayers on Yom Kippur, which says [SPEAKING HEBREW].
There are those that pay with their monies and there are those that pay with their lives.
And he said he paid with his monies.
They took his monies away, but they let them go.
And he told us to come back in a few days.
And indeed, we went back to our hometown.
And my father was already there.
The killing did not take place in our hometown.
In Krosno, there was no killing.
So my Uncle Haim was there.
The Jews were taking to work.

And as we got there and came back to our home, our store, the order came from the Gestapo, who had their headquarters across the street from us, that all Jews are to load up and go east on the other side of the San River, because, if you may remember, there was that secret agreement between Hitler and Stalin that they will divide Poland into two.
That border was only 40 kilometers, approximately.
The San River was the border.
And the Jews have to go there, so as to go to the Soviets, you know.
So we again took Mr.
Socha and we put our stuff.
And as we were loading, a Gestapo man comes down.
His name was Becker.
He comes down, says, no, [GERMAN], which means glazier master, you cannot go.
We need you here.
So we unloaded and we stayed.
Now, we were considered important to the economy, because you must know, there was lots of traffic.
There were broken windows everywhere.
Their car windows were smashed, many of them, that had to be glazed.
They had to be fixed.
So we were considered important for the economy.
So we stayed and they left us alone.
They took the-- the warehouse away and shipped it off to Germany, most of it.
Then they appointed a treuhander, which is a supervisor, to run the show.
And we were just working.
And we were very busy, really, very busy.
German soldiers were continually coming in to our store.
My-- the Gestapo used to come in to frame all their diplomas and all their things that they had that.
I can even remember the words that Schmatzler, who was Haupsturmfuhrer Schmatzler, who was the head of the Gestapo in our town, got this recognition from Hitler.
And it-- you know, it gave us-- I remember the words.
We framed that.
We framed for them Hitler's portrait by the dozen, you know, that they needed, and so on.
So they used to come to our house.
And you'll be amazed that this Schmatzler, for example, others, would come.
They would sit down, you know, while we were framing or doing-- fixing their stuff.
And they would talk to you just like I talk to you.
Oh, they would say, how many children do you have?
How are your children?
I mean, it's amazing.
You can only understand it if you read Christopher Browning's book, you know, These were ordinary men, but they were killers at the time.
These same men just went out and shot people ad lib, for nothing.
The same guys would come in and talk to you about the family.
So, anyway-- so we were quite busy.
And there was other Jews had to go to work, because a Jewish leadership was formed, a Judenrat, you know.
My father happened to be appointed also to be in the Judenrat.
And Mr.
Engel, who was from our town, but he lived in Germany-- and when Hitler chased the Polish Jews out of Germany, he came back to our home town.
He eventually became the head of the Judenrat.
He had to report to the Gestapo.
And they would have too-- they had to deliver, say, for example, they want 50 people here, 10 people to shovel snow, so many people to do this, to do this.
We were exempt, because we were busy working for the German Wehrmacht for the most part.
And of course, people weren't getting paid for this, you know.
Some-- some people were selling off whatever they could to survive.
And there was black marketeering going on, lots of it.
As a matter of fact, I had a heyday, because the German soldiers would come in and I could buy from them German script.
I don't know whether you will know what scripts are that the American military had in Europe, had different dollars they could exchange in Germany.
They also had different marks for the soldiers.
And there was a black market going on.
I used to buy those for Polish zlotys, and then I would sell it to another Jewish kid, you know, and I would-- for double that, you know.
So people were doing all these things.
Now, there was no shooting at that time, aside from that initial marching and killing that I just described to you.
Things were quiet.
It was hard, lots of hardship.
Schools were closed.
Believe it or not, schooling was going on.
We had a teacher coming and-- and teach us, my sister, me.
So we tried to live an ordinary life as best as we could.
There were no ghettos yet.
And then, of course, a month or two months later, Jews were ordered to wear the Star of David armband.
So we all had to wear that to be recognized.
Now, even so, we worked for Germans a lot, you know.
In all honesty, I have to say, I never heard an anti-Semitic word.
Never.
At no time.
One episode only, where a Sergeant asked me to take his-- his-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] basin that he washes, washing basin out and throw it out.
And I told him that wasn't what I was here for.
I was here to work glazing.
And he came over and he hit me over the face.
The other soldiers grabbed him and pulled him back.
And then he threatened me that he was going to report me to the Gestapo, you know.
The others pulled him back.
And a cousin of mine, a half-cousin of mine who used to work with us, Mr.
Heller, Moshe Heller, he pulled me away, gave me a hit sort of, you know, and things calmed down.
The only time that I personally have experienced any animosity from German soldiers.
And we worked for them.
And things were relatively quiet until-- here and there in 1940, '41, there would be a sporadic shooting of a Jew here, a Jew there.
Supposedly he didn't wear-- they caught him not wearing this, his armband, or something like this.
But it was relatively quiet.
Then came 1941.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The-- we knew that something was going on, because for a few weeks prior to the invasion of the Soviet Union-- Operation Barbarossa, that was the code name for the invasion of the Soviet Union-- there was lots of traffic going on.
We were extremely busy.
And you already saw slogans on the German trucks, like the [SPEAKING GERMAN].
That means the Russian pig is asking what isn't his.
That was a reference made that the Soviets wanted Romania, you know, Bukovina, and all of these countries, the Baltic countries and so on.
So we knew, all of a sudden, they're not kissing each other.
Before that, they, were lovey-bovey.
All of a sudden they hate each other.
There was lots of traffic going on.
The air base, in particularly-- in particular, was extremely busy.
Planes continually getting up, coming all the time.
It was busy.
And then, the war broke out, you know, the marching into the Soviet Union.

And to the east of us, all hell broke loose-- now, this didn't happen in our place-- for where the German armies entered, they now had organized their Sonderkommandos, the special commands, which were trained to kill Jews and Soviet functionaries, which--
Yeah?
OK.
Because, prior to that, there was lots of communication.
People would smuggle over back and forth.
It was very easy to go.
People would go back and forth, you know.
But-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and as they marched in there, many people who were caught up by the German armies now in former eastern Poland, now Soviet territory, came back.
So came my cousin back, the one that survived Auschwitz, and my other cousin, a number of them.
People came back.

[BACKGROUND NOISE] They wanted to come back to the German side even before that, believe it or not, because when the Germans were making-- were making a deal with Hitler to allow German nationals who lived in the eastern part of Poland and-- and in the Soviet Union-- to exchange them, you know, to bring them and let others go there, you know, Jews registered to come back, because it was relatively quiet.
We used to write to them.
We heard all the time from them.
They all wanted to come back, because the-- this killing wasn't there.
You see, they wanted to come home.
And as they registered, the Soviets took that the registration list, packed them on a train, and shipped them off to Siberia.
And that's-- my Uncle Joseph, with his family, was shipped off.
The one who is now in Australia, he stayed, but his sister was shipped off.

This cousin who was in the Polish army was shipped off and joined the Polish army when Stalin allowed the Polish government in exile to form an army and take them to the Middle East-- via the Middle East, and to fight on the Allied side.
That's how they were taken away, you see.
And so they all wanted to come back.
The Soviets packed them off.
And as they came back, they told of the atrocities, the Sonderkommandos.
For example, my cousin-- we had relatives in Stanislawow, which was a large city.
Something like, I believe close to 10,000 Jews were shot by these Sonderkommandos.
We heard all that.
But this did not occur where we were at.
So many of these people came back.
And life went on.
Now we heard of all the atrocities that were going-- that was going on in the east.
And then came 1941, December, 1941.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I remember it was around Hanukkah time.
Of course, the Germans [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] got stuck in the Soviet winter.
Initially, of course, they marched forward very rapidly.
Considering the roads in the Soviet Union, which were mud roads, you know, they rapidly moved eastward, very rapidly.
Within weeks they're already near Smolensk, near Leningrad, Kiev, and so on.
All of a sudden comes the winter.
Now they weren't prepared.
Hitler probably assumed that he will get the Ukrainians on this side, and by winter it will be over with.
But it wasn't.
So now the order came-- and I remember that evening.
The Judenrat was called to come up to the Gestapo.
And I stood at the window and watched them go up there, because the Gestapo was across the street from us.
And my father went up.
My father comes back, and he tells us the story.
The Gestapo from the town next to us, Jaslo, where there was the-- the headquarters of the Gestapo of that whole region, was in Jaslo, a town, 24 kilometers away from us.
And Schmatzler's group, in our town, was under them.
So what happened, my father came down, and he said, the order is that all Jews have to deliver all their woolens, all their furs, to one place within 24 hours.
Whoever is going to be caught who had not delivered that is going to be shot on the spot.
